interactions.summary: Jiang reads and interprets two Iliad episodes: Athena stopping Achilles from killing Agamemnon, and Achilles sending Patroclus into battle while preserving his own glory. He contrasts Homeric divine truth with flat modern factual prose.

claims.claim: Athena's intervention is not factual in a literal sense, but it truthfully describes an emotional command that stops Achilles from acting on murderous rage.
Modern prose can be factual and still less truthful because it describes behavior without revealing the spiritual-emotional force behind it.
claims.temporal_scope: General literary diagnosis made through Jiang's rewritten Achilles scene.

claims.claim: Achilles' exchange with Patroclus exposes his selfishness: even when the Greeks may be destroyed, he is concerned with humiliation, prizes, and his own glory.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's reading of Achilles before Patroclus enters battle.

claims.claim: Achilles permits Patroclus to fight only under conditions that direct honor back to Achilles, revealing that glory matters more to him than rescue alone.
claims.temporal_scope: Iliad scene as interpreted in the lecture.

signature_moments.moment: Athena is a hallucinated goddess and also the structure of self-command.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Only Achilles saw her... he would not fight the orders of Athena
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is Jiang's model of myth as psychological truth rather than fantasy.

signature_moments.moment: The Iliad is truthful but not factual; modern prose is factual but not truthful.
signature_moments.source_phrase: The Iliad is truthful, but not factual. Modern prose is factual, but not truthful.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This compact reversal is one of the episode's core thesis sentences.

signature_moments.moment: Achilles tells Patroclus to save the ships, but not to win too much glory.
signature_moments.source_phrase: You can win great honor, great glory for me
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It exposes the hidden ego inside a rescue command.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: In Jiang's reading, the divine figure who embodies the force that checks Achilles' rage.
A literary and metaphysical standard where myth reveals inner reality better than literal modern description.
Achilles' companion whose entry into battle reveals Achilles' self-protective desire for glory.

chronology_notes.note: This source sharply states Jiang's 2025 anti-modern literary position: spiritual myth may be more truthful than modern factual realism.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR likely mishears 'Myrmidons' as 'Meridians' and several Fagles lines, but Jiang's interpretive claim is clear.
